title: Whakamātau Taiao: Assessing the Health of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku | Environmental Impact Assessment of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Technology
culturalContext: Students will investigate the health of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, a significant stream that flows through our rohe and holds deep cultural significance for Ngāti Kahungunu. The awa is a traditional food source (mahinga kai) and features in local pūrākau about the taniwha Hinekōrako who protects its waters. Recent urban development has raised concerns about water quality and ecosystem health.
learningObjectives: Collect and analyze water quality data from three specific sites along Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku using digital monitoring tools
Design a culturally-informed technological solution that addresses one identified environmental impact on the awa
Present findings and proposed technological solutions using both scientific data and mātauranga Māori perspectives
Evaluate the potential effectiveness and cultural appropriateness of technological interventions for the awa

activities.title: Whakatau & Whakapapa o te Awa
activities.description: Begin with a karakia to acknowledge the awa. Local kaumātua will share the pūrākau of Hinekōrako and explain the cultural significance of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku as a mahinga kai. Students create a visual whakapapa (genealogy) map showing the connection between the awa, local hapū, and the college.

activities.title: Hautaū Wai - Water Quality Testing
activities.description: Using water testing kits and digital sensors, students collect data from three sites along the awa: near the source, behind the college, and downstream near urban development. They measure pH, nitrate levels, turbidity, and temperature, recording data digitally.

activities.title: Tātari Raraunga - Data Analysis Workshop
activities.description: In the technology lab, students use spreadsheet software to analyze their collected data, creating comparative graphs and identifying trends. They compare their findings with historical data provided by Hawke's Bay Regional Council.

activities.title: Hoahoa Hangarau - Technological Solution Design
activities.description: Working in small groups, students design a technological solution to address one identified issue (e.g., riparian planting drone mapping system, smart water quality monitoring buoy, educational app about the awa). Designs must incorporate both scientific and cultural considerations.

activities.title: Whakaaturanga - Community Presentation
activities.description: Groups present their technological solutions to an audience including kaumātua, representatives from Hawke's Bay Regional Council, and college staff, explaining how their design respects both environmental and cultural values of the awa.

resources: Kaumātua from local Ngāti Kahungunu marae (experts in pūrākau and mātauranga Māori of the awa)
Hawke's Bay Regional Council's 'State of the Environment' water quality reports and data sets
Digital water testing equipment (pH meters, turbidity tubes, temperature sensors) and tablets for data recording
NIWA's 'Stream Health Monitoring and Assessment Kit' (SHMAK) resources and guidelines

tasks: Water quality data collection and analysis report with comparative graphs
Design portfolio for technological solution including sketches, specifications, and cultural considerations
5-minute presentation to community panel explaining the technological solution

nzcAlignment: Technology Level 5: Technological Practice - Brief Development, Planning for Practice, Outcome Development and Evaluation
Technology Level 5: Technological Knowledge - Technological Modelling, Technological Products
Science Level 5: Living World - Ecology, and Planet Earth and Beyond - Earth Systems
Social Sciences Level 5: Place and Environment - Understand how people interact with natural and cultural environments
